items.section: A Benchmark for General AI Assistants
items.span_text: We introduce <sup>GAIA</sup>, a benchmark for General AI Assistants that, if solved, would represent a milestone in AI research. <sup>GAIA</sup> proposes real-world questions that require a set of fundamental abilities such as reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and generally tool-use proficiency. <sup>GAIA</sup> questions are conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs: we show that human respondents obtain 92% vs. 15% for GPT-4 equipped with plugins. This notable performance disparity contrasts with the recent trend of LLMs outperforming humans on tasks requiring professional skills in e.g. law or chemistry. <sup>GAIA</sup>’s philosophy departs from the current trend in AI benchmarks suggesting to target tasks that are ever more dificult for humans. We posit that the advent of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) hinges on a system’s capability to exhibit similar robustness as the average human does on such questions. Using <sup>GAIA</sup>’s methodology, we devise 466 questions and their answer. We release our questions while retaining answers to 300 of them to power a leader-board hereby accessible.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: Validation phase. After question creation, we ask two new independent annotators to answer the questions to check it is not ambiguous. We provide a typical annotator output for the validation phase in Table 2, as well as additional statistics on the validation phase of our protocol in Table 3. If the new annotators don’t fully agree with the original answer and there is no human error, the question is repaired if possible and removed otherwise.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: The annotators were also asked to answer the questions they created. We provide a typical example of annotated question in Table 1.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: We estimate the creation of a question, including its validation by two supplementary annotators and potential repairs, requires two hours of annotator time.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: Results. Our evaluation can be found in Figure 4, with more details in Table 4 (Appendix D.1). Our proposed levels of dificulty, loosely defined in terms of number of steps and number of diferent capabilities used, are correlated with the performance of current models, strengthening their validity. While humans excel at all levels, current best LLMs do poorly. Overall, <sup>GAIA</sup> allows to clearly rank capable assistants, while leaving a lot of room for improvement in the coming months and perhaps years.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: The discrepancy between GPT4 results without plugins and the others demonstrate that augmenting LLMs via tool APIs or access to the web improves answer accuracy, and unlock many new use cases, confirming the huge potential of this research direction. In particular, GPT4 + plugins exhibit behaviours such as backtracking or query refinement when the result is not satisfying, and relatively long plan execution. We provide examples of such behaviours in Appendix D.1. The discrepancy with humans suggests the work needed to fully unlock this potential.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: GPT4 plugins. As opposed to GPT4, there is currently no API for GPT4 with plugins, and we resort to manual ChatGPT queries. At the time of the writing, the user has to manually choose between an Advanced Data Analysis mode—with code execution and file reading capabilities—, and a set of at most three third party plugins. We use either the first mode or select third parties plugins according to our best guess of the most important capabilities given the task. We often rely on (i) a tool for reading various types of links, (ii) a web browsing tool, and (iii) a tool for computation. Sadly, it is currently not possible to use a stable set of plugins over some period of time as plugins often change or disappear from the store. Similarly, the oficial search tool for GPT4 was removed as it could possibly circumvent paywalls, before being recently brought back. Therefore, our score for GPT4 with plugins is an “oracle” estimate of GPT4 potential with more stable and automatically selected plugins rather than an easily reproducible result.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: <sup>1</sup>GPT4 does 86.4% on MMLU. Human non-specialist accuracy on the benchmark is only 34.5% Expert-level human performance is estimated at 89.8%.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: Increasing dificulty. The questions can be sorted into three levels of increasing dificulty depending on the number of steps required to solve the questions, and the number of diferent tools needed to answer the question. There is naturally not a single definition of step or tool, and possibly many paths to answer a given question. Therefore, we rely as a proxy on the number of steps and tools used by our annotators when crafting the questions. Figure 3 (right) illustrates the distribution of our questions along these two axes. Tools are always related to one or more capability (see Appendix C). We loosely use the following definitions to attribute a level to a question:
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: Question creation phase. We provided the annotators with a seed set of <sup>GAIA</sup> questions we devised ourselves, accompanied with the following instructions:
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: • Make sure your question is “interesting”, i.e. by reading it you think that an AI assistant answering this kind of question would help you a lot.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: • Make sure the answer to your question is a number or at most a few words to make evaluation robust.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: • Make sure the answer to your question does not change with time. This includes potential deletion of the source of truth.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: • Make sure the answer to your question is unambiguous.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text: • Make sure your question can be answered in a reasonable amount of time by a human annotator.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: This subsection delves into our question design and annotation process. In particular, we discuss some associated challenges and hope our insights will help the community building over<sup>GAIA</sup>.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: Description of capabilities. When answering the questions, annotators specified the steps that were followed and listed the tools they use. Based on the set of tools that were mentionned by the annotators, we defined capabilities required by <sup>GAIA</sup>. For each capability, we report examples of corresponding tool as reported by annotators.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: We provide the detailed scores of the diferent methods evaluated in Table 4.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: An illustration of these levels is provided in Figure 1. Those definitions are not hard constraints: for example, a question with less than 10 annotator steps but that requires complex web navigation might be categorised as Level 3 rather than 2. Our definition of the dificulty is validated in Section 4.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.section: D Extended description of our question design framework
items.span_text:
Table 3 Statistics on the validation phase. 623 newly crafted questions were validated by two new annotators each. The statistics were computed on their 1246 annotations. \*: a valid question is a question for which two annotators give the same answer as the question designer, or only one annotator gives the same answer as the question designer and the other made a mistake. \*\*: the human baseline is computed as the fraction of correct answers for all tentative on valid questions by the new annotators. Table 4 Score and average time to answer for various baselines on GAIA in %. \*: GPT4 + plugins scores were obtained by manually selecting plugins, and cannot be reproduced exactly for the reasons described in the main text. \*\*: Human score corresponds to the portion of correct answers by validation annotators for valid questions. Whenever we have direct API access, we run the model three times and report the average. Times for APIs were obtained by running the API on 20 questions then averaging, and were taken at a single point in time: they aren’t meant to reflect GPT4 vs. GPT4 Turbo speed, but GPT4 vs. other types of methods for answering <sup>GAIA</sup>.
Metric | Score in % (↑) | Avg. time to answer in mins (↓) | Level | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Number of questions | 146 | 245 | 75 | 146 | 245 | 75 | GPT4 | $9 . 1 \pm 2 . 5$ | $2 . 6 \pm 0 . 6$ | 0 | 0.19 | 0.15 | N.A. | GPT4 Turbo | $1 3 . 0 \pm 2 . 1$ | $5 . 5 \pm 1 . 4$ | 0 | 0.24 | 0.12 | N.A. | AutoGPT (GPT4 backend) | 14.4 | 0.4 | 0 | 7.6 | 11.7 | N.A. | GPT4 + plugins* | 30.3 | 9.7 | 0 | 0.65 | 0.53 | N.A. | Search engine | 7.4 | 0 | 0 | 7.4 | N.A. | N.A. | Human annotator** | 93.9 | 91.8 | 87.3 | 6.8 | 10.5 | 17.7
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: \- Level 1 questions generally require no tools, or at most one tool but no more than 5 steps.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: \- Level 2 question generally involve more steps, roughly between 5 and 10 and combining diferent tools is needed.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

items.span_text: \- Level 3 are questions for a near perfect general assistant, requiring to take arbitrarily long sequences of actions, use any number of tools, and access to the world in general.
items.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

final_answer.markdown:
## How GAIA was constructed

GAIA was built around real-world assistant questions that require reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and tool use, chosen so that they are "conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs" [1]. The authors devised 466 such questions and their answers, releasing the questions while holding back the answers to 300 of them to power a leaderboard [1].

**Annotator-driven pipeline (Section 3.4 / Appendix D).** The questions were written by human annotators, who were given a seed set of GAIA questions and a list of design constraints [2]. Each annotator was asked to:

- make the question "interesting" (an AI assistant answering it would be very useful) [3];
- restrict the answer to a number or at most a few words so evaluation is robust [4];
- ensure the answer does not change with time, including possible deletion of the source of truth [5];
- ensure the answer is unambiguous [6];
- ensure a human annotator can answer it in a reasonable amount of time [7].

Annotators were also required to answer the questions they created themselves [8]. The authors estimate that crafting a single question, including the validation step and any repairs, takes about two hours of annotator time [9].

**Validation phase.** After creation, every question is independently re-answered by two new annotators to check for ambiguity. If the new annotators do not fully agree with the original answer and there is no annotator error, the question is repaired if possible, otherwise removed [10]. In the validation phase reported in Table 3, 623 newly crafted questions were each answered by two additional annotators (1,246 annotations in total), and a human baseline was computed as the fraction of correct answers by the new annotators on valid questions [11].

**Three difficulty levels (Section 3.3).** Levels are defined by the number of steps required and the number of different tools involved, using the annotators' own traces as a proxy [12]:

- Level 1: generally no tools, or at most one tool and no more than 5 steps [13].
- Level 2: roughly between 5 and 10 steps, combining different tools [14].
- Level 3: for a near-perfect general assistant, requiring arbitrarily long action sequences, any number of tools, and general world access [15].

These are loose definitions, not hard constraints (e.g., a sub-10-step question requiring complex web navigation could still be Level 3) [16]. The final GAIA validation/test split reported in Table 4 contains 146 Level 1, 245 Level 2, and 75 Level 3 questions [11].

## Reported human vs. GPT-4-with-plugins result

The headline finding from the paper is that human respondents obtain **92%** versus **15%** for GPT-4 equipped with plugins [1]. Table 4 gives the per-level breakdown (number of questions: 146 / 245 / 75 at Levels 1/2/3) [11]:

| Method | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human annotator (validation) | **93.9%** | **91.8%** | **87.3%** |
| GPT-4 + plugins (oracle) | **30.3%** | **9.7%** | **0%** |

For context, the other systems in the same table scored: GPT-4 9.1 / 2.6 / 0; GPT-4 Turbo 13.0 / 5.5 / 0; AutoGPT (GPT-4 backend) 14.4 / 0.4 / 0; search engine 7.4 / 0 / 0 [11].

Two important caveats the authors flag for the GPT-4 + plugins number:

- It is treated as an **"oracle"** estimate because there is no API for GPT-4 with plugins, so plugins were **manually chosen** per question (Advanced Data Analysis vs. up to three third-party plugins), and the available plugins change over time [17].
- The human number is the score obtained by the validation annotators when re-answering the questions, and humans also pay a higher time cost (e.g., 6.8 / 10.5 / 17.7 minutes per question across the three levels, versus 0.65 / 0.53 / N.A. for GPT-4 + plugins) [11].

Sources
[1] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, A Benchmark for General AI Assistants, p. 1
[2] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[3] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[4] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[5] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[6] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[7] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[8] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[9] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[10] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[11] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 21
[12] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 3.3 Composition of GAIA, p. 6
[13] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 3.3 Composition of GAIA, p. 6
[14] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 3.3 Composition of GAIA, p. 6
[15] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 3.3 Composition of GAIA, p. 6
[16] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 3.3 Composition of GAIA, p. 6
[17] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, 4 LLMs results on GAIA, p. 8

locations.preview: LLMs, Human and Search engine scores and time to answer for GAIA Figure 4 Scores and time to answer per method and level. As stated in the main text, GPT4 + plugins score should be seen as an oracle since the plugins were chosen manually depending on the question. Human score refers to the score obtained by our annotators when validating the questions.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: AutoGPT4, which allows GPT4 to automatically use tools, ofer disappointing results for Level 2, and even Level 1 compared to GPT4 without plugins. This discrepancy might come from the way AutoGPT4 relies on the GPT4 API (prompt and generation parameters) and will require new evaluation in the near future. AutoGPT4 is also slow compared to other LLMs. Overall, the collaboration between a human and GPT4 with plugins seem to ofer the best ratio of score versus time needed so far.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: In search for more challenging benchmarks, current trend suggests to seek tasks that are ever more dificult for humans, and challenge LLMs with more intricate educational assessments, for example in STEM and Law, or target more complex realisations, such as writing a coherent book. But, tasks that are dificult for humans are not necessarily dificult for recent systems: the challenging MMLU or GSM8k benchmarks for example (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cobbe et al., 2021) are already close to be solved

Both figures are based on information reported by human annotators when answering the questions, and AI systems might proceed diferently.

items.span_text: Increasing dificulty. The questions can be sorted into three levels of increasing dificulty depending on the number of steps required to solve the questions, and the number of diferent tools needed to answer the question. There is naturally not a single definition of step or tool, and possibly many paths to answer a given question. Therefore, we rely as a proxy on the number of steps and tools used by our annotators when crafting the questions. Figure 3 (right) illustrates the distribution of our questions along these two axes. Tools are always related to one or more capability (see Appendix C). We loosely use the following definitions to attribute a level to a question:

locations.preview: Results. Our evaluation can be found in Figure 4, with more details in Table 4 (Appendix D.1). Our proposed levels of dificulty, loosely defined in terms of number of steps and number of diferent capabilities used, are correlated with the performance of current models, strengthening their validity. While humans excel at all levels, current best LLMs do poorly. Overall, <sup>GAIA</sup> allows to clearly rank capable assistants, while leaving a lot of room for improvement in the coming months and

locations.preview: Partial versus full automation. While partial automation of a process still requires humans in the loop, full automation completely removes that need. Systems that respectively allow partial automation and full automation can be as close as a few percentage of error on a given task—the former would have say 1% and the latter 0%—, yet yield these two fundamentally diferent paradigms. Full automation is a goal that deep learning has been striving to achieve, without complete success to date: in sp

locations.preview: AutoGPT4, which allows GPT4 to automatically use tools, ofer disappointing results for Level 2, and even Level 1 compared to GPT4 without plugins. This discrepancy might come from the way AutoGPT4 relies on the GPT4 API (prompt and generation parameters) and will require new evaluation in the near future. AutoGPT4 is also slow compared to other LLMs. Overall, the collaboration between a human and GPT4 with plugins seem to ofer the best ratio of score versus time needed so far.

Table 3 Statistics on the validation phase. 623 newly crafted questions were validated by two new annotators each. The statistics were computed on their 1246 annotations. \*: a valid question is a question for which two annotators give the same answer as the question designer, or only one annotator gives the same answer as the question designer and the other made a mistake. \*\*: the human baseline is computed as the fraction of correct answers for all tentative on valid questions by the new annotators. Table 4 Score and average time to answer for various baselines on GAIA in %. \*: GPT4 + plugins scores were obtained by manually selecting plugins, and cannot be reproduced exactly for the reasons described in the main text. \*\*: Human score corresponds to the portion of correct answers by validation annotators for valid questions. Whenever we have direct API access, we run the model three times and report the average. Times for APIs were obtained by running the API on 20 questions then averaging, and were taken at a single point in time: they aren’t meant to reflect GPT4 vs. GPT4 Turbo speed, but GPT4 vs. other types of methods for answering <sup>GAIA</sup>.

arguments.markdown:
## How GAIA was constructed

GAIA was built around real-world assistant questions that require reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and tool use, chosen so that they are "conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs" [[ev_cc589bd061af6a6d]]. The authors devised 466 such questions and their answers, releasing the questions while holding back the answers to 300 of them to power a leaderboard [[ev_cc589bd061af6a6d]].

**Annotator-driven pipeline (Section 3.4 / Appendix D).** The questions were written by human annotators, who were given a seed set of GAIA questions and a list of design constraints [[ev_cd6bb5f911234d42]]. Each annotator was asked to:

- make the question "interesting" (an AI assistant answering it would be very useful) [[ev_90ff2c9cd5d5eecd]];
- restrict the answer to a number or at most a few words so evaluation is robust [[ev_dff5893eb18a1323]];
- ensure the answer does not change with time, including possible deletion of the source of truth [[ev_280366de5f8a83bf]];
- ensure the answer is unambiguous [[ev_8f57f3c8c62618ef]];
- ensure a human annotator can answer it in a reasonable amount of time [[ev_3446a0ae710166d3]].

Annotators were also required to answer the questions they created themselves [[ev_a4a558e9b514dd80]]. The authors estimate that crafting a single question, including the validation step and any repairs, takes about two hours of annotator time [[ev_e1ed274349d01b41]].

**Validation phase.** After creation, every question is independently re-answered by two new annotators to check for ambiguity. If the new annotators do not fully agree with the original answer and there is no annotator error, the question is repaired if possible, otherwise removed [[ev_a99ac3580fe7f61e]]. In the validation phase reported in Table 3, 623 newly crafted questions were each answered by two additional annotators (1,246 annotations in total), and a human baseline was computed as the fraction of correct answers by the new annotators on valid questions [[ev_17d5e99b5e4902eb]].

**Three difficulty levels (Section 3.3).** Levels are defined by the number of steps required and the number of different tools involved, using the annotators' own traces as a proxy [[ev_d8c13921b995e8b4]]:

- Level 1: generally no tools, or at most one tool and no more than 5 steps [[ev_787d210d2c7478c4]].
- Level 2: roughly between 5 and 10 steps, combining different tools [[ev_882aea63557875af]].
- Level 3: for a near-perfect general assistant, requiring arbitrarily long action sequences, any number of tools, and general world access [[ev_d947de8b7fcb3be8]].

These are loose definitions, not hard constraints (e.g., a sub-10-step question requiring complex web navigation could still be Level 3) [[ev_ac10b80faa437519]]. The final GAIA validation/test split reported in Table 4 contains 146 Level 1, 245 Level 2, and 75 Level 3 questions [[ev_17d5e99b5e4902eb]].

## Reported human vs. GPT-4-with-plugins result

The headline finding from the paper is that human respondents obtain **92%** versus **15%** for GPT-4 equipped with plugins [[ev_cc589bd061af6a6d]]. Table 4 gives the per-level breakdown (number of questions: 146 / 245 / 75 at Levels 1/2/3) [[ev_17d5e99b5e4902eb]]:

| Method | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human annotator (validation) | **93.9%** | **91.8%** | **87.3%** |
| GPT-4 + plugins (oracle) | **30.3%** | **9.7%** | **0%** |

For context, the other systems in the same table scored: GPT-4 9.1 / 2.6 / 0; GPT-4 Turbo 13.0 / 5.5 / 0; AutoGPT (GPT-4 backend) 14.4 / 0.4 / 0; search engine 7.4 / 0 / 0 [[ev_17d5e99b5e4902eb]].

Two important caveats the authors flag for the GPT-4 + plugins number:

- It is treated as an **"oracle"** estimate because there is no API for GPT-4 with plugins, so plugins were **manually chosen** per question (Advanced Data Analysis vs. up to three third-party plugins), and the available plugins change over time [[ev_1e617842e24a0e5d]].
- The human number is the score obtained by the validation annotators when re-answering the questions, and humans also pay a higher time cost (e.g., 6.8 / 10.5 / 17.7 minutes per question across the three levels, versus 0.65 / 0.53 / N.A. for GPT-4 + plugins) [[ev_17d5e99b5e4902eb]].

research_answer.markdown:
## How GAIA was constructed

GAIA was built around real-world assistant questions that require reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and tool use, chosen so that they are "conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs" [1]. The authors devised 466 such questions and their answers, releasing the questions while holding back the answers to 300 of them to power a leaderboard [1].

**Annotator-driven pipeline (Section 3.4 / Appendix D).** The questions were written by human annotators, who were given a seed set of GAIA questions and a list of design constraints [2]. Each annotator was asked to:

- make the question "interesting" (an AI assistant answering it would be very useful) [3];
- restrict the answer to a number or at most a few words so evaluation is robust [4];
- ensure the answer does not change with time, including possible deletion of the source of truth [5];
- ensure the answer is unambiguous [6];
- ensure a human annotator can answer it in a reasonable amount of time [7].

Annotators were also required to answer the questions they created themselves [8]. The authors estimate that crafting a single question, including the validation step and any repairs, takes about two hours of annotator time [9].

**Validation phase.** After creation, every question is independently re-answered by two new annotators to check for ambiguity. If the new annotators do not fully agree with the original answer and there is no annotator error, the question is repaired if possible, otherwise removed [10]. In the validation phase reported in Table 3, 623 newly crafted questions were each answered by two additional annotators (1,246 annotations in total), and a human baseline was computed as the fraction of correct answers by the new annotators on valid questions [11].

**Three difficulty levels (Section 3.3).** Levels are defined by the number of steps required and the number of different tools involved, using the annotators' own traces as a proxy [12]:

- Level 1: generally no tools, or at most one tool and no more than 5 steps [13].
- Level 2: roughly between 5 and 10 steps, combining different tools [14].
- Level 3: for a near-perfect general assistant, requiring arbitrarily long action sequences, any number of tools, and general world access [15].

These are loose definitions, not hard constraints (e.g., a sub-10-step question requiring complex web navigation could still be Level 3) [16]. The final GAIA validation/test split reported in Table 4 contains 146 Level 1, 245 Level 2, and 75 Level 3 questions [11].

## Reported human vs. GPT-4-with-plugins result

The headline finding from the paper is that human respondents obtain **92%** versus **15%** for GPT-4 equipped with plugins [1]. Table 4 gives the per-level breakdown (number of questions: 146 / 245 / 75 at Levels 1/2/3) [11]:

| Method | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human annotator (validation) | **93.9%** | **91.8%** | **87.3%** |
| GPT-4 + plugins (oracle) | **30.3%** | **9.7%** | **0%** |

For context, the other systems in the same table scored: GPT-4 9.1 / 2.6 / 0; GPT-4 Turbo 13.0 / 5.5 / 0; AutoGPT (GPT-4 backend) 14.4 / 0.4 / 0; search engine 7.4 / 0 / 0 [11].

Two important caveats the authors flag for the GPT-4 + plugins number:

- It is treated as an **"oracle"** estimate because there is no API for GPT-4 with plugins, so plugins were **manually chosen** per question (Advanced Data Analysis vs. up to three third-party plugins), and the available plugins change over time [17].
- The human number is the score obtained by the validation annotators when re-answering the questions, and humans also pay a higher time cost (e.g., 6.8 / 10.5 / 17.7 minutes per question across the three levels, versus 0.65 / 0.53 / N.A. for GPT-4 + plugins) [11].

Sources

[2] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19

[8] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
[9] GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants, D Extended description of our question design framework, p. 19
